Hello. i want to be able to have a bigger number than uint64, now im  thinking is there a way to add an uint128 and int128 to NumPy?

As @rgommers mentioned that we have an experimental cross-platform float-128 (backed by sleef) and we did test it on some of the applications, you can read more about it in the mentioned blog-post. Given its currently experimental so some operations are not supported yet (like matmul) so yeah feedbacks are always welcome.
For f2py as far I know sleef does not have any fortran bindings yet (rust and julia are supported) but it can be used via ISO_C_BINDING in fortran

+{% set name = "numpy_quaddtype" %}
+{% set version = "0.0.4" %}
+{% set python_req = "python >=3.10,<3.13" %}
+
+package:
+  name: {{ name }}
+  version: {{ version }}
+
+source:
+  url: https://github.com/SwayamInSync/numpy-user-dtypes/archive/refs/tags/quaddtype-v{{ version }}.tar.gz
+  sha256: a400f35cf980965486f9dd00c9d2289e877a4bd09a848f49b82de23e1855beea
+
+build:
+  number: 0
+  script: |
+    cd quaddtype
+    {{ PYTHON }} -m pip install . -vv --no-build-isolation
+
+requirements:
+  build:
+    - {{ compiler('c') }}
+    - {{ compiler('cxx') }}
+    - {{ stdlib("c") }}
+    - python
+    - pip
+    - meson
+    - meson-python
+    - setuptools
+    - sleef ==3.6.1
+    - numpy >=2.0
+  host:
+    - {{ python_req }}
+    - pip
+    - setuptools
+    - meson
+    - meson-python
+    - sleef ==3.6.1
+    - numpy >=2.0
+  run:
+    - {{ python_req }}
+    - {{ pin_compatible('sleef') }}
+    - {{ pin_compatible('numpy') }}
+
+test:
+  imports:
+    - numpy_quaddtype
+  requires:
+    - {{ python_req }}
+    - numpy >=2.0
+    - pytest
+  source_files:
+    - quaddtype/tests
+  commands:
+    - pytest quaddtype/tests  # [not linux]","path":"recipes/numpy_quaddtype/meta.yaml","commit_id":"8a5135529cc3a8c877da52efaea027ff6f1eea09","original_commit_id":"d1bb396c5c96be52a2ce806dcfa56c120498f839","user":{"login":"SwayamInSync","id":74960567,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc0OTYwNTY3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/74960567?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync","html_url":"https://github.com/SwayamInSync","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/SwayamInSync/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Wierdly, one test was failing on linux so this is temporarily off

```python
def test_array_operations():
    arr1 = np.array(
        [QuadPrecision("1.5"), QuadPrecision("2.5"), QuadPrecision("3.5")])
    arr2 = np.array(
        [QuadPrecision("0.5"), QuadPrecision("1.0"), QuadPrecision("1.5")])

    result = arr1 + arr2
    expected = np.array(
        [QuadPrecision("2.0"), QuadPrecision("3.5"), QuadPrecision("5.0")])
    assert np.all(result == expected)
```
Will be doing some debugging on the conda's Linux environment to see where it is actually failing

Here are some more details about this specific migrator: > > TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot > PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details). > > The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy > version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy > will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions. > > Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a > run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a > run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove > any occurrences of this. > > However, you will still need to add the lower bound for the numpy version, > in line with what the upstream package requires. The default lower bound from > the run-export is `>=1.19`; if your package needs a newer version than that, > please add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`. > > Finally, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package > is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should > check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you > need to add a `- numpy <2.0dev0` run requirement until that happens (check numpy > issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages). > > ### To-Dos: > * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility) > * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2.0dev0` upper bound under `run:`. > * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, double-check their supported numpy versions. > * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you need to add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`. > * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed. > > PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that > the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until > a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this > feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in > case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).

{"repository_id":875132237,"push_id":20832019010,"size":3,"distinct_size":3,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"50644c5e8a50ee274d73dc371ca0c49185aab527","before":"430bc0d6a6cf23689294a2b9bce164c50a1aba62","commits":[{"sha":"b7015223c0a91feb99f1f13da64a2a01b0ba8901","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"Rebuild for numpy 2.0

TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot
PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details).

The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy
version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy
will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions.

Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a
run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a
run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove
any occurrences of this.

However, you will still need to add the lower bound for the numpy version,
in line with what the upstream package requires. The default lower bound from
the run-export is `>=1.19`; if your package needs a newer version than that,
please add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`.

Finally, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package
is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should
check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you
need to add a `- numpy <2.0dev0` run requirement until that happens (check numpy
issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages).

### To-Dos:
  * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility)
    * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2.0dev0` upper bound under `run:`.
    * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, double-check their supported numpy versions.
    * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you need to add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`.
  * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed.

PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that
the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until
a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this
feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in
case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/numpy_quaddtype-feedstock/commits/b7015223c0a91feb99f1f13da64a2a01b0ba8901"},{"sha":"e27e129bda3ced5439dee79dd398ab5cd2d80caa","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"MNT: Re-rendered with conda-build 24.9.0, conda-smithy 3.43.0, and conda-forge-pinning 2024.10.21.14.45.36","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/numpy_quaddtype-feedstock/commits/e27e129bda3ced5439dee79dd398ab5cd2d80caa"},{"sha":"50644c5e8a50ee274d73dc371ca0c49185aab527","author":{"email":"39274aa59c0e86ece7035d781ab8049836047f8a@users.noreply.github.com","name":"Swayam"},"message":"Merge pull request #1 from regro-cf-autotick-bot/rebuild-numpy2-0-1_ha145f0","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/numpy_quaddtype-feedstock/commits/50644c5e8a50ee274d73dc371ca0c49185aab527"}]}

> Once that's done and we have wheels up on PyPI I think we can make an announcement on the numpy mailing list asking for feedback.

Yeah it checks if dtype is not the subclass of `numeric.floating` then it tries to convert it into numpy floats, which leads to KeyError
```
if not issubclass(dtype, numeric.floating):
--> 524     newdtype = _convert_to_float[dtype]
    525     if newdtype is not dtype:
    526         # dtype changed, for example from complex128 to float64
    527         dtypes.append(newdtype)

KeyError: <class 'numpy.object_'>
```
Here is `_convert_to_float` dictionary
```
_convert_to_float = {
    ntypes.csingle: ntypes.single,
    ntypes.complex128: ntypes.float64,
    ntypes.clongdouble: ntypes.longdouble
    }
 ```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2294623083/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> 
> In any case, PPC is going to run into [conda-forge/status#185](https://github.com/conda-forge/status/issues/185). Since you're already cross-compiling osx-arm64, the hope would be that you could switch to cross-compilation also for linux-{aarch64,ppc64le}, or in the worst case, emulate them on azure (which is quite a bit slower, but since the build times here are not that big, that would also be an option).

Thanks @h-vetinari , switched to azure provider
```yaml
provider: {linux_aarch64: azure, linux_ppc64le: azure}
```

Travis CI is still failing though, but is it now to a point where we can merge it?
